summary: The author secured 1st place by ensembling ModernBERT-base, CodeBERT, AutoGluon, and Uni-Mol 2 models trained separately for each target property. The winning strategy hinged on three core innovations: post-processing predictions to correct a severe systematic distribution shift in the leaderboard test labels, pretraining BERT models on pseudolabeled PI1M data using a multi-task ranking classifier, and rigorously cleaning external datasets with dirty labels via isotonic rescaling, error filtering, and Optuna-tuned sample weighting. The pipeline combined extensive tabular feature engineering, molecular dynamics simulations for descriptor generation, and strict train/test deduplication to prevent leakage. These technical choices collectively drove significant score improvements across all properties.
extensive_summary:
## Ensemble Architecture & Post-Processing
The final solution combines property-specific predictions from ModernBERT-base, CodeBERT, AutoGluon, and Uni-Mol 2 84M. A critical breakthrough was identifying a systematic bias in the leaderboard test labels. By probing the public LB score curve, the author derived a constant offset coefficient (0.5644) and applied it to the glass transition temperature predictions: `submission_df["Tg"] += (submission_df["Tg"].std() * 0.5644)`. This simple post-processing step dramatically improved both public and private LB scores.

## External Data Cleaning & Handling Dirty Labels
Multiple external datasets were integrated, but they contained label noise, constant biases, and outliers. The author implemented a five-step cleaning pipeline: (1) isotonic regression to rescale raw labels against a strong ensemble's predictions, (2) error-based filtering to discard samples exceeding a threshold relative to the mean absolute error, (3) Optuna-tuned sample weighting to downweight lower-quality datasets, (4) semi-manual threshold filtering for suspicious values, and (5) model stacking where 41 XGBoost models predicted MD simulation results to generate supplemental features for AutoGluon.

## Deduplication & Leakage Prevention
Exact duplicates within training data were removed by converting SMILES to canonical form and dropping the lower-weighted sample. To prevent train/test leakage, Tanimoto similarity scores were computed between training monomers and test folds, dropping any training example with a similarity > 0.99. The author noted that exact canonical SMILES deduplication was likely sufficient, but kept the stricter filtering due to paranoia from a prior CV bug.

## BERT Pretraining & Finetuning
BERT models underwent a two-stage process. First, they were pretrained on 50,000 PI1M polymers using a multi-task ranking classification objective inspired by the RankUp paper, which reduced exposure to label noise from pseudolabels. Second, they were finetuned with AdamW, one-cycle LR, AMP, and a dual learning rate strategy (higher LR for the regression head). Data augmentation used non-canonical SMILES generation (10x during training, 50x during TTA with median aggregation).

## Tabular Models & Feature Engineering
Tabular features spanned RDKit descriptors (Morgan, atom pair, topological-torsion, MACCS, graph features), backbone/sidechain features (+107 AI-generated variants), MD simulation descriptors (FFV, density, Rg, 3D descriptors), polyBERT embeddings, and Gasteiger charge statistics. AutoGluon's "best" quality preset significantly outperformed a manually tuned XGBoost/LightGBM/TabM ensemble with far less compute. MD-derived features improved GBDT models but were detrimental to AutoGluon.

## 3D Models & Model Selection Insights
Uni-Mol 2 84M required minimal tuning but suffered from OOM issues on large monomers and poor FFV performance. The author found that general-purpose models (ModernBERT) outperformed chemistry-specific ones, larger models did not help, and code-pretrained models (CodeBERT) beat natural language-pretrained ones. GNNs (D-MPNN) and GMM-based augmentation were explicitly noted as ineffective.
competition_flow: Raw SMILES and external datasets were cleaned, deduplicated, and augmented, then fed into separate BERT, AutoGluon, and Uni-Mol pipelines with extensive tabular feature engineering and MD-derived descriptors, followed by property-specific ensembling and a constant-bias post-processing step to correct test label distribution shifts.
data_processing: Tanimoto similarity filtering (>0.99) to prevent train/test leakage
Error-based data filtering using MAE ratios
Semi-manual threshold filtering for suspicious values
LAMMPS equilibrium simulation for property estimation
features_engineering: Morgan, atom pair, and topological-torsion fingerprints
Backbone vs. sidechain features (+107 AI-generated variants)
MD simulation descriptors (FFV, density, Rg, 3D descriptors)
Element composition and bond type ratios
XGBoost ensemble predictions on MD results
cv_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation on the host's original training data (80% train, 20% test per fold), with all selected external data included in training. CV scores were computed exclusively on the held-out host data to prevent test leakage.
ensembling: Property-specific predictions from ModernBERT-base, CodeBERT, AutoGluon, and Uni-Mol 2 were combined, with Tg predictions adjusted by a constant bias offset scaled by the prediction standard deviation to correct for a systematic test label distribution shift.
insights: General-purpose foundation models outperformed chemistry-specific ones for this polymer prediction task.
Larger model sizes did not guarantee better performance and sometimes degraded scores.
Models pretrained on code corpora yielded better results than those pretrained on natural language.
AutoGluon's automated tuning significantly outperformed manually constructed tabular ensembles with far less compute.
MD simulation-derived features improved GBDT models but were detrimental to AutoGluon.
critical_findings: The leaderboard test labels contained a severe systematic bias that required a constant offset post-processing step to align predictions with the ground truth.
Training on external datasets with dirty labels required a multi-step cleaning pipeline to extract useful signal rather than discarding them entirely.
Exact-duplicate canonical SMILES deduplication was sufficient to prevent leakage, making Tanimoto similarity filtering largely redundant.
MD simulation data generation was highly unstable and required a LightGBM classifier to predict and route problematic polymers to faster configurations.

papers.title: RankUp: Boosting Semi-Supervised Regression with an Auxiliary Ranking Classifier
